raw_case:
A rare case of pediatric T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia with myeloid mimicry
Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) with granular blasts was historically referred to as “granular ALL”, but this is not recognized as a distinct entity in the current WHO classification. This rare morphological feature is predominantly associated with a B-cell immunophenotype, while T-ALL with prominent cytoplasmic granules is extremely rare, with limited available genetic, molecular, and prognostic data. Here, we report a unique case of ALL in a 9-year-old girl. Initial blood tests revealed leukocytosis, and both peripheral blood and bone marrow morphology showed a high number of blasts with granular cytoplasm and bilobed nuclei. Immunophenotyping confirmed T-cell origin. The patient tested positive for KMT2A::AFDN, with WT1 overexpression, and NRAS and EZH2 mutations were detected by next-generation sequencing (NGS). Remarkably, the patient achieved complete remission after treatment and has shown no signs of relapse for nearly four years. Her favorable response to conventional therapy underscores the importance of molecular phenotyping in the treatment of this disease. The continued use of NGS to gather relevant molecular data is crucial for further understanding the molecular phenotype and prognosis of such atypical ALL cases.

Supplementary Information

The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s12672-024-01600-1.

Introduction

Azurophilic granules in the cytoplasm of leukemic blasts are typical features of acute myeloblastic leukemia. Rarely, however, cases of acute ALL may present with significant numbers of cytoplasmic granules in blasts that may be so prominent as to suggest a myeloid differentiation. ALL cases presenting with significant numbers of cytoplasmic granules in blasts have been historically termed as “granular ALL” in literature from the 1980s to early 1990s [1–4]. While this term is not specifically defined in the current World Health Organization classification of hematopoietic and lymphoid neoplasms, the morphological feature of prominent cytoplasmic granules in ALL blasts remains a recognized, albeit rare, phenomenon. The reported prevalence of “granular ALL” is 1.7–7% of all ALL cases with a predominant precursor B-cell immunophenotype, L2 morphology and a variable prognosis [5]. Some studies have conducted ultrastructural studies of these blasts and described these granules variably as membrane bound vesicles, parallel tubular arrays, collection of virus like-vesicles, detached masses of nuclear chromatin and detached nuclear clubs [4]. ALL with granular blasts has been occasionally reported in pediatric ALL variants, accounting for 4.5% of all pediatric ALL cases [6]. It is important to note that T-Lineage ALL with granular blasts is extremely rare, and the genetic characteristics and outcomes of pediatric cases of it have not been well described or analyzed to date. Here, we report a rare pediatric case of T-Lineage ALL with prominent cytoplasmic granules specific genetic characteristics and discuss the impact of these features on clinical outcomes through a review of the literature.

Case presentation

A 9-year-old girl was hospitalized for leukocytosis. Physical examination did not reveal palpable lymphadenopathy, and no hepatosplenomegaly was found. Complete blood cell count revealed white blood cells, 71.61 × 109/L; red blood cells, 4.54 × 1012/L; hemoglobin, 126 g/L; and platelets, 72 × 109/L. Peripheral blood (PB) film showed 83% abnormal cells with granular cytoplasm and irregular or bilobed nuclei (Fig. 1). Bone marrow aspirate showed 98% abnormal cells with prominent granular cytoplasm and round or bilobed nuclei (Fig. 2A, B). Myeloperoxidase was negative, glycogen was strongly positive (Fig. 2C). Flow cytometry analysis revealed that approximately 83.6% of nucleated cells exhibited a unique immunophenotype (Fig. 3) (Detailed results are shown in Supplementary Fig. 1). These cells predominantly expressed T-cell associated antigens, including CD7 (partial), CD2, CD8 (partial), CD4 (partial), and cCD3, suggesting T-cell lineage differentiation. Several notable immunophenotypic features warranted careful consideration: nTDT negativity was unusual for typical T-ALL; CD1a negativity excluded cortical T-ALL; and the absence of CD34, CD117, and HLA-DR distinguished these leukemic cells from very early progenitors. Importantly, the leukemic cells co-expressed myeloid-associated antigens, including CD33, CD13 (partial), and CD15. The cells were positive for CD99 and negative for CD5, CD19, CD10, CD20, CD56, cMPO, and cCD22. Therefore, the flow cytometry results suggest an atypical T-All with partial myeloid antigen expression. Cytogenetics revealed an abnormal complex karyotype 47,XX, + 6,add(6)(q11) × 2, + 7,del(11)(q23),der(12)t(12;17)(p11;q11),-17[9]/46,XX[2] (Fig. 2D). Targeted NGS analysis of 26 genes, including ATM, CARD11, CDKN2A, CREBBP, DNMT3A, EZH2, FBXW7, FLT3, IKZF1, IL7R, JAK1, JAK3, KDM6A, NOTCH1, NRAS, NT5C2, PHF6, PTEN, PTPN11, RUNX1, SETD2, STAT3, STAT5B, TP53, TPMT, and WT1, was performed. The analysis detected two mutations: NRAS (NM_00254:c.35G > C(p.G12A)exon2, variant allele frequencies 47% (2010X)) and EZH2 (NM_004456:c.839C > T(p.S280F)exon8, variant allele frequencies 28.4% (2930X)). The fusion gene KMT2A::AFDN and overexpression of WT1 were confirmed by reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction. The final diagnosis was T-ALL.The patient underwent multiple phases of treatment (Fig. 4), achieving significant results and complete remission (CR). Phase I: Following diagnosis, the patient received a prednisolone pre-trial. On day 5, complete blood count showed a white blood cell count of 177.34 × 109/L, hemoglobin 110 g/L, and platelets 58 × 109/L. To prevent tumor lysis syndrome, prednisolone was discontinued, and hydroxyurea was administered. From days 6 to 29, the patient received VDLD (vincristine, daunorubicin, l-asparaginase, dexamethasone) induction therapy. From days 44 to 58, the patient underwent CAM chemotherapy (cyclophosphamide, cytarabine, 6-mercaptopurine). From days 59 to 67, the patient developed soft tissue swelling in the left calf due to a fungal infection, which was managed symptomatically. Phase II: From days 69 to 83, the patient underwent another round of CAM chemotherapy. On day 74, to prevent central nervous system leukemia, the patient received intravenous methotrexate and intrathecal chemotherapy. Phase III: Two weeks after discharge, the patient received BLOCK1 (Dexamethasone 10 mg from days 1 to 5; vincristine 1.5 mg on day 1 and day 6; high-dose methotrexate 2500 mg on day 1; cyclophosphamide 150 mg from days 2 to 4; cytarabine 1000 mg on day 5; PEG-asparaginase 2500 U on day 6; intradural therapy: methotrexate 12mg + Cytarabine 30 mg + dexamethasone 2.5 mg on day 1) and BLOCK2 chemotherapy (Dexamethasone 15 mg from days 1 to 5; vindesine 2 mg on day 1 and day 6; high-dose methotrexate 5000 mg on day 1; ifosfamide 800 mg from days 2 to 4; daunorubicin 30 mg on day 5; PEG-asparaginase 2500 U on day 6; intradural therapy: methotrexate 12mg + cytarabine 30mg + dexamethasone 2.5 mg on day 1).

Discussion

ALL with granular blasts is rare and predominantly associated with a precursor B-cell immunophenotype, while T-cell ALL with prominent cytoplasmic granules is extremely uncommon and has been scarcely reported (Supplementary Table 1). There are even fewer reports of studies analyzing such unique ALL phenotypes in children using NGS, but this technology may prove beneficial for diagnosing and assessing the prognosis of such patients.The WT1 gene is well-known to hematologists, often being overexpressed in 75–100% of Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) patients [7–9]. In ALL, researchers have also detected WT1 overexpression in a significant number of cases [10–12], suggesting its potential as a therapeutic target. Data show no significant differences in WT1 expression between T-ALL and B-ALL subtypes [12]. However, studies that separately analyzed these subtypes yielded controversial results, with some indicating higher WT1 overexpression in B-ALL and others in T-ALL [13, 14]. Some researchers attribute these discrepancies to the predominance of studies on adult ALL patients or heterogeneous populations of children and adults, and possibly the use of PCR techniques less sensitive to WT1 detection in PB samples [12]. Additionally, a study by Chen et al. suggested that WT1 levels at diagnosis appear higher in children with T-ALL compared to those with B-ALL, consistently exceeding reference limits [15]. Regarding the correlation between WT1 overexpression in ALL and prognosis, Tosello et al. found no adverse prognosis associated with WT1 mutations in pediatric and adult T-ALL cases [16]. It is noteworthy to note that these conclusions are not specifically drawn from ALL cases with granular blasts. However, in this case, the implication is that WT1 may follow a similar expression pattern, potentially showing incremental expression with limited impact on achieving complete remission (CR) and relapse in patients.In the study by Wiemels et al. [17], the detection rate of RAS gene mutations in 191 children with ALL was 20%. Other reports indicate that the incidence of NRAS gene mutations is approximately 6% [18], with point mutations commonly found at codons 12, 13, and 61 [18, 19]. In this case, the NRAS mutation occurred at codon 12, a common mutation site. Additionally, we found some conflicting results; some studies suggest that the RAS gene mutation rate is higher in children with B-ALL compared to those with T-ALL [20], while other teams have confirmed no difference in the RAS gene mutation rate between B-ALL and T-ALL [18]. RAS gene mutations are found in over two-thirds of ETP-ALL cases, but only in 19% of non-ETP-ALL cases [21]. Due to the flow cytometry results showing that the abnormal cell population was partially expressed CD8, the diagnosis does not fully meet the criteria for ETP-ALL. Thus, we speculate that the NRAS mutation in this case may not represent a common molecular phenotype in ALL. However, it remains unclear whether the mutation rate is higher in T-ALL, requiring further large-scale studies for clarification. Regarding prognosis, a study involving 870 ALL patients showed that RAS gene mutations had no impact on event-free survival, disease-free survival, or overall survival rates [19]. Case et al. also reported [22] that the mutation rate of the RAS gene is similar at diagnosis and relapse in children with ALL, indicating that RAS gene mutations are not a risk factor for ALL relapse.Mutations in EZH2 have been found in various cancers, including several types of B-cell and T-cell-related lymphoid malignancies, and are associated with poor clinical prognosis and outcomes. In most lymphoid malignancies, EZH2 is overexpressed and oncogenic, but it acts as a tumor suppressor in T-ALL [23].In this case, our patient responded well to conventional treatment after diagnosis, achieving sustained CR. During our follow-up, the patient remained in stable condition with no signs of relapse. We believe this is closely related to their molecular phenotype.According to a large-scale study of 3,401 cases with KMT2A rearrangements, KMT2A::AFDN fusion was identified in both ALL and AML, with varying frequencies across different age groups and lineages. Specifically, this fusion was found in 2.1% of ALL cases (46/2182) and 8.1% of AML cases (91/1116), with notably higher frequencies in pediatric ALL (5.8%, 31/530) and adult AML (10.2%, 45/441) [24]. While traditionally associated with monocytic differentiation in AML-M4/M5a [25], these epidemiological data suggest that KMT2A::AFDN fusion can occur across the spectrum of acute leukemias. Zhu et al. found that KMT2A::AFDN-positive cells exhibit typical monocytic leukemia characteristics, such as nuclear convolutions and folds, 1–2 large and prominent nucleoli, semi-transparent cytoplasm with fine pink granules, and negative peroxidase staining [26].The morphological and immunophenotypic features observed in this case provide interesting diagnostic considerations, while also highlighting the complexity of leukemia classification. From a cytomorphological perspective, the presence of azurophilic granules in the cytoplasm is one of the primary distinguishing features between AML and ALL. However, it has been shown that these cytoplasmic granules can also be found in ALL blasts. Historically, the morphological characteristic of “granular ALL” was defined as having more than 5% of lymphoblasts with multiple cytoplasmic granules larger than 0.5 μm in diameter [5]. Although rare, some studies suggest that ALL cases with prominent cytoplasmic granules may have a higher incidence in pediatric patients and may be associated with a poorer prognosis in this cohort [1]. However, it should be noted that due to the rarity of T-ALL cases with this morphological feature, most previous prognostic conclusions have been derived from studies on B-ALL with cytoplasmic granules or studies that did not differentiate ALL subtypes. Thus, there is currently no convincing evidence indicating a poorer prognosis specifically for pediatric T-ALL cases with prominent cytoplasmic granules. In this case, the prominent morphological features of lymphoblasts, characterized by abundant coarse granules within the cytoplasm, some of which cover the nucleus, along with the presence of some lymphoblasts with bilobed nuclei, may lead to confusion with certain AMLs such as acute promyelocytic leukemia, acute basophilic leukemia, and large granular lymphocytic leukemia.Additionally, although partial expression of CD8 traditionally does not conform to the WHO classification of ETP-ALL [27], recent studies have shown that ETP-ALL can exhibit heterogeneous immunophenotypic features. Notably, a scoring system proposed by Haitham et al. suggests a more nuanced approach to ETP-ALL diagnosis [28]. The presence of myeloid antigens (CD33, CD13) alongside CD5 negativity and absence of CD1a in our case aligns with some key features of ETP-ALL, although the partial expression of CD8 presents an atypical feature. Furthermore, the lack of specific myeloid markers is sufficient to exclude mixed phenotype acute leukemia [29]. Therefore, this case, as an atypical T-ALL with myeloid antigen expression, may represent a distinct borderline entity, highlighting the complexity of leukemia classification and the need for ongoing refinement of diagnostic criteria.The cytogenetic analysis revealed several complex chromosomal abnormalities, including monosomy 17 and del(11)(q23), which warrant further discussion. While monosomy 17 typically suggests a potential loss of one copy of the TP53 gene, targeted NGS analysis of TP53 in our case showed no pathogenic variants or copy number alterations. The apparent discrepancy between the del(11)(q23) finding and the detection of KMT2A::AFDN fusion by RT-PCR is particularly intriguing. This observation aligns with previous reports by Mansur et al., who described unbalanced KMT2A/AFDN fusions involving deletions of both KMT2A and AFDN genes [30–32]. Indeed, some KMT2A rearrangements, particularly KMT2A/AFDN fusions, can involve more complex mechanisms than simple reciprocal translocations, including cryptic insertion events that may not be readily apparent through conventional cytogenetic analysis alone. This case adds to the growing evidence that molecular testing is essential for accurate detection and characterization of genetic alterations in acute leukemia, as some structural changes may be more complex than they appear by conventional karyotyping.An intriguing question regarding its potential molecular basis is that, while it is currently not possible to definitively establish a direct causal relationship between the KMT2A::AFDN fusion gene and the observed cellular features based on a single case, it is worth considering potential mechanistic links. The AFDN gene encodes the multi-domain protein Afadin, which functions as a scaffold protein connecting the actin cytoskeleton to Nectin receptors, playing a crucial role in the formation of intercellular junctions [26]. The disruption of this gene through fusion with KMT2A could potentially alter cellular architecture and adhesion properties. Moreover, KMT2A rearrangements are known to impact gene expression profiles and cellular differentiation programs, and related acute leukemias often exhibit lineage plasticity [33, 34]. This molecular background may explain the observed cellular phenotype that bridges T-ALL and myeloid features. This case adds to the growing evidence that some acute leukemias exist along a biological continuum rather than fitting precisely into discrete categories. While meeting the technical criteria for T-ALL classification, the presence of the KMT2A::AFDN fusion and mixed phenotypic features suggests a more complex biological entity. This observation aligns with recent understanding that genetic alterations can drive unique phenotypic patterns that challenge traditional classification schemes. Further research, including functional studies and analysis of additional cases with this specific fusion, would be valuable in understanding how these molecular alterations influence lineage determination and cellular phenotype in acute leukemia.

Conclusion

In conclusion, this case highlights the evolving complexity in acute leukemia classification, particularly when cases present with overlapping features that challenge traditional diagnostic boundaries. While the predominant T-lymphoid immunophenotype allows for classification as T-ALL under current criteria, the presence of prominent cytoplasmic granules, partial myeloid antigen expression, and the KMT2A::AFDN fusion gene suggests a more complex biological entity. Such cases underscore the importance of comprehensive diagnostic evaluation incorporating morphological. As our understanding of the molecular landscape of acute leukemia continues to expand, cases like this may contribute to the refinement of classification systems to better reflect the biological continuum of these diseases. Further investigation of similar cases may provide insights into the mechanisms underlying lineage plasticity and potentially inform more precise diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 9-year-old girl  
- **Chief Complaint:** Hospitalized for leukocytosis  
- **History of Present Illness:** None provided  
- **Past Medical History:** None provided  
- **Personal History:** None provided  
- **Family History:** None provided  
- **Physical Examination:** No palpable lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly  
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Blood Tests:** White blood cells 71.61 × 10^9/L, hemoglobin 126 g/L, platelets 72 × 10^9/L  
  - **Peripheral Blood Film:** 83% abnormal cells with granular cytoplasm and bilobed nuclei  
  - **Bone Marrow Aspirate:** 98% abnormal cells with granular cytoplasm, bilobed nuclei, myeloperoxidase negative, glycogen strongly positive  
  - **Flow Cytometry:** 83.6% cells expressed T-cell antigens (CD7, CD2, CD8, CD4, cCD3), co-expressed myeloid antigens (CD33, CD13, CD15), and showed atypical T-ALL immunophenotype  
  - **Cytogenetics:** Complex karyotype with chromosomal abnormalities, including the KMT2A::AFDN fusion gene, WT1 overexpression, and NRAS and EZH2 mutations detected by next-generation sequencing  
  - **Other Tests:** RTPCR confirmed KMT2A::AFDN fusion
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Consideration of Acute Leukemias:** Presentation with leukocytosis and high abnormal blast count raised suspicion for acute leukemia. Morphological features, including granular blasts, suggested potential myeloid leukemia.
   - **Exclusion of Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML):** Negative myeloperoxidase staining and lack of key myeloid markers like cMPO and CD34 ruled out AML despite granular morphology.

2. **Differentiation within Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia Subtypes:**  
   - **Exclusion of B-ALL:** Immunophenotyping ruled out B-cell lineage (CD19, CD10, and CD20 negative).  
   - **Consideration of T-ALL:** Strong expression of T-cell markers (CD7, CD2, cCD3) with absence of CD1a and CD5 indicated atypical T-cell ALL. Granular blasts remained an atypical feature for T-ALL.  

3. **Mixed-Phenotype Acute Leukemia (MPAL) Consideration:** Co-expression of myeloid antigens (CD13, CD33, CD15) raised suspicion for MPAL. However, lack of specific myeloid markers (CD34, CD117, HLA-DR) excluded this diagnosis.

4. **Incorporation of Molecular Findings:** Detection of KMT2A::AFDN fusion gene, NRAS and EZH2 mutations, and WT1 overexpression further supported the diagnosis of atypical T-ALL.  

5. **Final Diagnosis as Atypical T-ALL:** The unique immunophenotypic and molecular findings, along with the morphological presentation, were sufficient to classify this as T-ALL with granular blast morphology.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Atypical T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL) with prominent granular blast morphology, partial myeloid antigen expression, and KMT2A::AFDN fusion.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL) with KMT2A::AFDN fusion

results.content:
> Reasoning
**Addressing the patient's needs**
OK, let me see. The goal is to understand the patient's case, identify challenges, and collaborate effectively to uncover potential solutions.
**Assessing and diagnosing**
Considering the patient's symptoms and history, I began to form an idea of potential diagnoses, aiming to identify the most plausible one to guide further diagnostic steps.
**Coordinating care**
I'm working with the medical team to provide comprehensive patient care. This involves assessing symptoms, coordinating treatments, and ensuring continuity of care across various departments.
**Charting the course**
I'm piecing together the treatment strategy for the patient, focusing on their symptoms and the necessary comprehensive diagnostic and therapeutic steps to ensure optimal care delivery.
**Assessing current findings**
I'm working through the patient's diagnosis and treatment plan, ensuring it considers their symptoms, medical history, and any recent tests, aiming to tailor the approach for optimal care.
**Laying out the options**
I'm pulling together a comprehensive assessment based on the patient's symptoms and history. This helps in narrowing down the most plausible diagnoses to establish a clear treatment plan.
**Assessing symptoms and history**
I'm mapping out the patient's symptoms and medical history, aiming to pinpoint plausible diagnoses and establish the best treatment approach for comprehensive care.
**Evaluating symptoms**
OK, let me see. I’m focusing on the patient's symptoms and history to determine the optimal diagnosis approach. This involves thorough observation, examination, and patient-centered communication.
Reasoned for 7 seconds

### Reasoning:
<step 1> The patient's primary symptom is leukocytosis, indicated by a white blood cell count of 71.61 × 10^9/L, which is significantly elevated. This suggests an underlying hematological issue, potentially a blood disorder such as leukemia.

<step 2> The peripheral blood film shows 83% abnormal cells with granular cytoplasm and bilobed nuclei, which is highly suggestive of an abnormal hematopoietic cell population. These features are often seen in cases of leukemia, specifically in myeloid or mixed-lineage disorders.

<step 3> The bone marrow aspirate revealing 98% abnormal cells with similar features (granular cytoplasm, bilobed nuclei) further supports the hypothesis of a hematological malignancy. The myeloperoxidase negativity and glycogen positivity are noteworthy, as these findings point to an acute leukemia with mixed myeloid and lymphoid features.

<step 4> Flow cytometry reveals that the majority of the cells express T-cell markers (CD7, CD2, CD8, CD4, cCD3), along with myeloid markers (CD33, CD13, CD15), indicating a mixed immunophenotype, which is atypical for standard T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (T-ALL). This suggests a complex or mixed lineage leukemia, combining both T-lymphoid and myeloid features.

<step 5> Cytogenetic analysis and next-generation sequencing revealed a complex karyotype with abnormalities, including the KMT2A::AFDN fusion gene, WT1 overexpression, and mutations in NRAS and EZH2. These genetic findings point toward a high-risk leukemia, particularly KMT2A-associated leukemia, which is often seen in cases of mixed lineage leukemias and is associated with poor prognosis.

<step 6> RTPCR confirmed the presence of the KMT2A::AFDN fusion, further confirming the diagnosis of a leukemia involving this fusion gene, which is characteristic of specific hematologic malignancies such as mixed-lineage leukemias or acute leukemias with T-lymphoid and myeloid features.

### Answer:
Mixed-phenotype acute leukemia with KMT2A::AFDN fusion gene.
